想問好多問題但又好chur 咁

1. When did you settle there? How well did you adapt
2. Any advice for general programming for investing? (Sth like study journey)
3. Any advice for career switching (assume that I also have a bba regree) to a data scientist/sth related to programming/data (ie steps and degrees)

replies.msg:
No problem. By the way I don't know how to type Chinese, I can only hand write it on my phone so It would be faster for me to type in English.

Q1. I came to Melbourne with my parent after the handover, about 20 years ago. I am 31 years old. So I did everything in Melbourne, from school to university until I got a job in Sydney last year.

Q2. Depends on what you want to do. Most sell side and buy side firm would use either C++ or Python. For investment you would mostly be doing things like factor modelling or portfolio optimisation for low frequency strategies, so Python or R are popular. However coding is like a side skill, you'll need to have good knowledge on statistics and machine learning. The developers look after the actual implementation.

Q3. Remember, data scientist are scientists, not coders. So you'll need a solid background in statistics as machine learning theory, especially supervised learning, is basically statistics. I would say take a course that gives you good training in statistics, you will probably need to do some undergraduate statistics as well as I doubt business statistics is up for the task (sorry to be blunt).

Also in data science, both tech and finance, you'll be up against many good candidates, often with PhDs and interviews are very technical in general. So don't assume you can hand wave anything.

想問好多問題但又好chur 咁

1. When did you settle there? How well did you adapt
2. Any advice for general programming for investing? (Sth like study journey)
3. Any advice for career switching (assume that I also have a bba regree) to a data scientist/sth related to programming/data (ie steps and degrees)

No problem. By the way I don't know how to type Chinese, I can only hand write it on my phone so It would be faster for me to type in English.

Q1. I came to Melbourne with my parent after the handover, about 20 years ago. I am 31 years old. So I did everything in Melbourne, from school to university until I got a job in Sydney last year.

Q2. Depends on what you want to do. Most sell side and buy side firm would use either C++ or Python. For investment you would mostly be doing things like factor modelling or portfolio optimisation for low frequency strategies, so Python or R are popular. However coding is like a side skill, you'll need to have good knowledge on statistics and machine learning. The developers look after the actual implementation.

Q3. Remember, data scientist are scientists, not coders. So you'll need a solid background in statistics as machine learning theory, especially supervised learning, is basically statistics. I would say take a course that gives you good training in statistics, you will probably need to do some undergraduate statistics as well as I doubt business statistics is up for the task (sorry to be blunt).

Also in data science, both tech and finance, you'll be up against many good candidates, often with PhDs and interviews are very technical in general. So don't assume you can hand wave anything.

Q1. I came to Melbourne with my parent after the handover, about 20 years ago. I am 31 years old. So I did everything in Melbourne, from school to university until I got a job in Sydney last year.

Q2. Depends on what you want to do. Most sell side and buy side firm would use either C++ or Python. For investment you would mostly be doing things like factor modelling or portfolio optimisation for low frequency strategies, so Python or R are popular. However coding is like a side skill, you'll need to have good knowledge on statistics and machine learning. The developers look after the actual implementation.

Q3. Remember, data scientist are scientists, not coders. So you'll need a solid background in statistics as machine learning theory, especially supervised learning, is basically statistics. I would say take a course that gives you good training in statistics, you will probably need to do some undergraduate statistics as well as I doubt business statistics is up for the task (sorry to be blunt).

Also in data science, both tech and finance, you'll be up against many good candidates, often with PhDs and interviews are very technical in general. So don't assume you can hand wave anything.
